input.prompt: My ASP.NET Core (.NET 8) app running on a Windows Server has slow requests under load. I have admin access. Which trace should I collect to capture waits/blocks, and what PerfView arguments should I use?

data.content: My ASP.NET Core (.NET 8) app running on a Windows Server has slow requests under load. I have admin access. Which trace should I collect to capture waits/blocks, and what PerfView arguments should I use?
data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-04-29T01:20:14.585Z</current_datetime>

My ASP.NET Core (.NET 8) app running on a Windows Server has slow requests under load. I have admin access. Which trace should I collect to capture waits/blocks, and what PerfView arguments should I use?

input.initialPrompt: My ASP.NET Core (.NET 8) app running on a Windows Server has slow requests under load. I have admin access. Which trace should I collect to capture waits/blocks, and what PerfView arguments should I use?

data.content:
Collect a **Thread Time (wall-clock) trace** — it captures CPU, waits, locks, and I/O blocks, not just on-CPU time. This is the right choice for diagnosing slowness under load.

**PerfView command:**

```
PerfView /threadTime /nogui /accepteula /zip /merge /maxCollectSec:30 collect
```

Key flags:

| Flag | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `/threadTime` | Enables context-switch / wall-clock profiling (captures waits & blocks) |
| `/nogui` | Headless — safe for production servers |
| `/maxCollectSec:30` | Limits collection to 30 s (adjust to your repro window) |
| `/zip /merge` | Produces a single portable `.zip` for offline analysis |

**Analysis in PerfView:**

1. Open the `.zip`, go to **Thread Time Stacks**.
2. Set the process to your app (`dotnet` or your exe name).
3. Look at **BLOCKED_TIME** and **CPU_TIME** columns — the stacks show exactly where threads are waiting (lock contention, async awaits, DB calls, etc.).

**Optional — add .NET runtime events for GC/contention detail:**

```
PerfView /threadTime /nogui /accepteula /zip /merge /maxCollectSec:30 /clrEvents:GC,Contention,Exception collect
```

This adds GC pauses, `Monitor.Enter` contention, and first-chance exceptions to the trace without significant overhead.
data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about performance tracing for an ASP.NET Core (.NET 8) app on Windows Server with slow requests under load. They want to know which trace to collect and what PerfView arguments to use. This is a knowledge question — no tools needed beyond reporting intent.

Let me give a concise, actionable answer.
